topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. Liam shifted his weight on the creaky floorboards, his palms immediately slick with a cold sweat. He’d been tasked with clearing out the spare room, a chore he’d been dreading all week. Tucked inside an old wooden chest, beneath moth-eaten blankets, he found it: a meticulously organized stack of every assignment from middle school. His breath hitched. Each crisp sheet, each neatly folded worksheet, felt like a tiny accusation. He stared at the sheer volume, his throat tightening. *How could she keep all of this?* A tremor ran through his hands, making the papers tremble precariously in his grasp. He needed to leave, needed to escape the suffocating weight of this unexpected discovery.

[story 2]

"Mom?" Maya whispered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the old refrigerator. She stood in the kitchen, her gaze fixed on the overflowing filing cabinet. It wasn't the clutter of everyday mail; this was an archive. Hundreds of graded tests, essays, and project drafts, all perfectly cataloged. Her stomach churned, a tight, nauseating knot forming just beneath her ribs. She reached out, her fingers brushing against a particularly vibrant blue notebook. The texture of the paper felt strangely charged. Every movement she made felt overly deliberate, as if she were afraid of disturbing the fragile order. A quick, shallow inhale was all she could manage before she spun around, needing the solid reality of the hallway to anchor her.

[story 3]

I found the box behind the linen closet, and the sheer volume of schoolwork hit me like a physical blow. My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. It was all there—the proofs, the poetry submissions, the messy science reports—each one accounted for. I picked up a geometry worksheet, and the edges felt sharp under my fingertips. My vision tunneled slightly. I couldn't seem to focus on anything else; the silence of the house amplified the frantic drumming inside my chest. I backed away slowly, my feet feeling heavy and clumsy on the hardwood floor, desperately wanting to dissolve into the shadows. This secret felt too big, too heavy to hold.
